segments.s179-g57: سوی ... گرفتند راه is a compact, somewhat poetic order: سوی + place gives direction ('toward the city of Iran'), and گرفتند (past, 3rd-person plural) combines with راه (road) to mean 'they took the road' or 'they set out.' In classical Persian the verb can follow the directional phrase and its object without an explicit subject (the plural verb implies 'they').
segments.s179-g58: This is a coordinated list of forces/figures following the previous clause: names and nouns are simply conjoined with و 'and.' In this context زواره and فرامرز are personal names, پیل is the elephant (a war-elephant), and سپاه names the army/host accompanying them.
segments.s179-g59: به پیش functions adverbially as 'ahead' or 'in front'; اندرون means 'inside' or 'within' (here locating Zal relative to the formation), and the proper name زال follows; با انجمن means 'with his assembly/retinue.' Word order places the locational phrase before the person: 'ahead, inside, Zal with his retinue.'
segments.s179-g60: درفش بنفش is a noun + adjective (banner + 'purple'), and از پس + place indicates origin or position 'from behind/after Piltan.' In poetry از پس often means 'from behind' or 'coming after,' so the phrase says the purple banner followed/appeared from behind Piltan (and after the elephants).
segments.s179-g61: پس here is the temporal 'then.' آگاهی آمد بر + indirect object means 'news/information came to' (i.e., 'news reached the king'); آمد is past tense, and بر شهریار marks the recipient with the preposition بر ('upon/to the king'). The whole clause is an impersonal construction meaning 'the king received word.'
segments.s179-g62: که introduces a content clause 'that ...' describing the news. آمد (past) is the main verb 'came,' ز + ره is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from/on the road' or 'along the road,' and پهلوان سوار places the noun 'champion/hero' with سوار as a following adjective/participial noun 'mounted' (i.e., a mounted hero arrived).
segments.s179-g63: A list of personal names: here زواره and فرامرز are proper names; the conjunction و links them to دستان and سام. دستان in this list is functioning as a name (not its literal meaning), so it is part of the enumeration of persons.
segments.s179-g64: بزرگان is the predicate nominal 'great ones' referring back to the named men. که هستند is a relative clause 'who are,' with هستنـد the present plural copula agreeing with the plural list. با جاه و نام means 'with rank and name' — here 'با' expresses possession or accompaniment, جاه = rank/status, and نام means reputation/fame.
segments.s179-g81: خروش آمد is a compact past-tense narration: noun خروش (a loud cry/uproar) + آمد (came/occurred). The coordinating و links the next noun phrase ناله بوق و کوس (the lament/cry of horn and drum); word order is noun + verb for the occurrence, then noun phrases conjoined by و.
segments.s179-g82: ز marks origin/source (poetic use = 'from'); قلب سپه is a genitive sequence ('the heart/core of the army/host') with the possessor following the possessed. The three proper names گیو و گودرز و طوس are coordinated by و and are the source/center from which the sound issues.
segments.s179-g83: به here marks direction/toward ('to/toward'); پیش‌گو is the noun 'seer/soothsayer' and پیلتن is a following name/place — together they form the indirect object/destination. راندند is past plural 'they drove' (transitive), so the whole phrase means 'they drove (to) the seer Piltan.'
segments.s179-g84: به شادی is an adverbial phrase meaning 'with joy' (به + noun used adverbially). برو is an imperative/hortative 'go' used here as an exclamation; آفرین is an acclamation 'bravo,' and خواندند (past plural) governs both items — 'they cried/sang/uttered' those exclamations.
segments.s179-g85: گرفتند is past plural 'they seized/took.' هر سه is a quantified phrase 'each/all three' (here meaning 'all three of them'). ورا is an old pronominal adverb/accusative meaning 'them' or 'to them' and در کنار is a locative phrase 'to the side/beside' expressing where they placed them.
segments.s179-g86: بپرسید here is the narrative past 'he/they asked' (third-person past). The following name شیراوژن is the subject in preposed position, and از introduces the person being asked (شهریار) meaning 'asked of/from the king' (i.e., he put a question to the king).
segments.s179-g87: ز marks origin ('from Rostam'), سوی is a directional preposition 'toward' and is followed by the coordinated names زال سام ('Zal and Sam') as the destination or addressees. آمدند is the past plural 'they came' and closes the clause.
segments.s179-g88: گشاده دل is a compound adjective meaning 'open‑hearted' (گشاده modifying دل), and شادکام is another adjectival predicate 'fortunate/joyful.' آمدند repeats the past plural verb for emphasis — both adjectives describe the manner/condition in which they arrived.
segments.s179-g105: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression: نگه (gaze) + کرد (past of کردن) = 'looked'. In poetic word order the verb precedes the subject, so نگه کرد رستم = 'Rostam looked.' سرو پای اوی is an epithet phrase: سرو ('cypress', used metaphorically for a tall/beautiful person) + پای ('at the foot/of the foot') + اوی (old form of the pronoun; here possessive 'his') = 'the cypress at his feet' (i.e. the cypress‑limbed person associated with him).
segments.s179-g106: نشست is past 'sat/settled' (here an intransitive past). The sequence 'نشست و سخن گفتن و رای اوی' uses parataxis (verbs and verbal nouns linked by و) so actions are listed without repeating auxiliary verbs: 'sat, spoke words, and (gave) his counsel.' سخن گفتن is a verb phrase 'to say/speak words', and رای اوی ('the counsel of him') uses اوی as possessive 'his' with رای 'counsel/opinion' (verb 'gave' is implied).
segments.s179-g107: رخش is the steed's name or 'the steed'; گشت is past of گشتن used copulatively 'became/turned (into)'. پرخون is an adjectival compound 'full of blood/bloodied'. The clause continues with 'و دل پر ز درد' where دل 'heart' + پر 'full (of)' + ز 'from/because of' + درد 'pain/sorrow' = 'and his heart full of pain.'
segments.s179-g108: زکار is a prepositional phrase 'ز' + کار meaning 'because of/on account of (the) deed/affair'; سیاوش is the name Siyāvash. بسی here functions as an adverb 'much/many' (classical usage), یاد کرد is the verbal phrase 'remembered' (یاد 'remembrance' + کرد 'did' → 'remembered').
segments.s179-g109: به شاه جهان گفت = 'he said to the king of the world': به marks the indirect object/recipient, شاه جهان is a compound 'king of the world'. گفت is the past verbal root 'said' (poetic diction may treat it compactly), and کای شهریار is a vocative: کای 'O' + شهریار 'sovereign/king.'
segments.s179-g110: جهان را تویی is a topical/exclamatory structure: جهان 'the world' + را (topic/definite marker) + تویی (archaic/dialectal 'thou art') → 'You are (for) the world' i.e. 'you are the world's (ruler)'. از پدر یادگار is a genitival/qualifying phrase: از 'from/of' + پدر 'father' + یادگار 'memento/legacy' = 'a remembrance/legacy of your father.'
segments.s179-g111: ندیدم is negative past 'I did not see' (ن- prefix + دیدم). من is the emphatic pronoun 'I' (optional but present for emphasis). اندر جهان is an archaic prepositional phrase 'in the world'. تاج‌ور is a compound 'tājvar' meaning 'crown‑bearer' (= crowned ruler) and functions as the object of 'ندیدم' (I have not seen such a king).
segments.s179-g112: بدین is an archaic demonstrative/prepositional combination 'به + این' meaning 'with this/so great'; فر is the technical Zoroastrian/royal term 'farr' (royal glory). و links 'فر' to مانندگی پدر, where مانندگی 'likeness/ressemblance' + پدر 'father' = 'likeness to (his) father'. Together: 'a crown‑bearer with this (such) glory and resemblance to his father.'
segments.s179-g97: به رستم چنین گفت is a simple past report clause: به + name marks the indirect object/recipient ('to Rostam'), چنین ('thus/so') sets the manner, and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. کای پهلوان is a vocative: کای = 'O' (addressing someone) + پهلوان 'champion/hero'. The vocative follows the reported clause here, a poetic word order.
segments.s179-g98: همیشه بدی شاد و روشن‌روان is a benedictory wish directed at the addressee: همیشه 'always' adverbially, بدی here functions as an archaic/poetic subjunctive or jussive form meaning 'may you be' (not the common noun 'evil'), followed by two predicative adjectives شاد 'happy' and روشن‑روان 'bright‑souled'. The coordinating و links the two adjectives.
segments.s179-g99: به گیتی خردمند و خامش تویی is a verbless equational clause with تویی 'you are' as the copula. به گیتی ('in/within the world') sets the domain; خردمند و خامش are two coordinated adjectives 'wise and quiet/sober'. Word order places the locative/phrase before the predicate for emphasis, and تویی finishes the clause as 'you are'.
segments.s179-g100: که پروردگار سیاوش تویی is a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که 'that/who', giving a reason or identity: پروردگار سیاوش 'the protector/nurturer of Siavash' is the predicate, and تویی again is the copula 'you are'. The clause explains/clarifies the preceding praise.
segments.s179-g101: سر زال زان پس به بر در گرفت is temporally ordered: زان پس 'after that/thereupon' is an adverbial, and گرفت (past of گرفتن) is the main verb 'took'. سر زال here functions as the affected body‑part phrase ('his/ Zāl's head' or 'he took him to Zal's chest/bosom' in context) and به بر در indicates direction/location ('to/on the chest/into the arms'); the phrase is idiomatic/poetic and the exact case marking is compressed, typical of epic diction.
segments.s179-g102: ز بهر پدر دست بر سر گرفت: ز بهر is an archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of' or 'because of', followed by پدر 'the father' as beneficiary/grounding. دست بر سر گرفت is an idiom 'took/laid (his) hand upon the head' — here indicating a gesture of blessing or respect. گرفت is again the past verb 'took/placed'.
segments.s179-g103: گوان را به تخت مهی برنشاند is a transitive past clause: را is the definite/direct‑object marker attached to گوان (here meaning 'thus/so' or indicating the person placed), به تخت مهی 'onto the moon‑like throne' gives direction/location, and برنشاند (past) means 'placed/caused to sit'. The order (object before verb) is typical in Persian with را-marked objects.
segments.s179-g104: بریشان همی نام یزدان بخواند: بریشان is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + ایشان) meaning 'on/for them' or 'over them'; همی is an emphatic/progressive particle here, نام یزدان 'the name of God' is the object, and بخواند is a past tense verb meaning 'he recited/called (upon)'. The clause means he invoked God's name on their behalf.
segments.s179-g41: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The clause has the names جم و فریدون as its agents and the verb بیاراست (past) meaning 'adorned' or 'arrayed'; گاه is the object/goal 'the age' or 'the time' and follows the verb in this poetic word order.
segments.s179-g42: ز ... و ز ... is a repeated prepositional/causal marker 'in/with/regarding' (poetic ز) linking two spheres of action: داد 'justice' and بخشش 'giving/granting'. نیاسود is the past negative of آسودن, 'did not rest/was not at rest,' and شاه (the subject) comes after this predicate in the terse, inverted poetic order.
segments.s179-g43: جهان شد is a subject + copular/venitive construction 'the world became'; پر از is the adjective + preposition 'full of', so پر از خوبی و ایمنی = 'full of goodness and security.' شد is the perfective/become auxiliary here.
segments.s179-g44: ز بد marks cause or source 'from/because of evil'; بسته شد is a passive/perfect expression 'was bound/closed' (بسته as participial/adjectival form + شد), and دست اهریمنی is the noun phrase 'the demonic hand' (دست 'hand/power' qualified by the adjective/attributive اهریمنی).
segments.s179-g45: فرستادگان (envoys/messengers) is the subject and آمد is the past verb 'came'. از هر سوی is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction' where سوی here means 'side/direction' rather than 'to'.
segments.s179-g46: ز هر نامداری و هر پهلوی repeats the z + هر pattern to enumerate sources: ز هر نامداری ('from every renown/notable source' — here نامداری is used abstractly to imply famous persons/houses) and هر پهلوی ('every side/quarter' where پهلوی = side/region).
segments.s179-g47: پس is an adverb 'then'; آگاهی here means 'information/report' and آمد is 'arrived/came'. سوی marks the destination 'to/toward', and نیمروز is used as a place-name (the region Nimruz) rather than the temporal 'midday' in this context.
segments.s179-g48: بنزد is an archaic/prepositional compound meaning 'to (the presence of)' and governs the following dative phrase. سپهدار is a title 'commander' and گیتی‌فروز is an epithet/name (literally 'world-illuminating') identifying that commander.
segments.s179-g33: به هر جای ویرانی is a prepositional phrase marking the target/location (به + هر + جای + ویرانی = 'to/into every ruined place'). آباد کرد is a past-tense causative/perfective construction: آباد (past participle/adjective 'made prosperous') + کرد (past 3rd‑sg. 'did/made'), so literally 'made [it] prosperous' = 'rebuilt/rehabilitated'. The order is poetic but standard: location phrase before the verb phrase.
segments.s179-g34: دل غمگنان is a genitive/possessive construction where غمگنان ('the sorrowful/sad ones') modifies دل ('hearts'): 'the hearts of the sorrowful.' از غم marks the source ('from grief'). آزاد کرد is again adjective + کرد: آزاد (adjective 'free') with کرد (past 3rd‑sg. 'made/did'), so 'made free' = 'freed'. The verb comes after its object/phrase in this poetic line.
segments.s179-g35: از ابر بهاران is a source phrase ('from the clouds of spring'); ببارید is the past tense (3rd sg.) of باریدن 'to rain/fall in drops'. نم (dew/moisture) is the thing that fell. Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb ببارید before its direct object نم).
segments.s179-g36: ز روی زمین uses poetic ز ('from') + روی زمین ('the face/surface of the earth'). زنگ غم is a metaphorical noun phrase 'the zāng (stain/rust) of grief' (غم modifies زنگ), and بزدود is the past 3rd‑sg. of a verb meaning 'removed/cleansed.' The line therefore says he removed the stain/rust of grief from the earth's surface; word order is compacted poetically (object phrase and modifier appear around the verb).
segments.s179-g37: جهان گشت is subject 'the world' + past verb گشت ('became'). پر (predicate adjective 'full [of]') governs the following complements: سبزه ('green growth/greenery') and رود آب ('rivers of water' — a common collocation). The structure is 'The world became full of X and Y.'
segments.s179-g38: سر غمگنان is 'the heads/minds of the sorrowful' (a possessive grouping). اندر آمد literally 'came into' (اندر = poetic 'in'), and به خواب indicates the state 'into sleep.' This is an idiomatic/periphrastic way to say 'sleep came upon them' or 'the sorrowful fell asleep.' آمد is past 3rd‑sg.
segments.s179-g39: زمین is the subject 'the earth.' چون بهشتی is a simile/comparison 'like a paradise' (چون = 'like/as'), and شد آراسته is a past construction where شد ('became') combines with آراسته (past participle 'adorned') to mean 'became adorned' or 'was made beautiful.'
segments.s179-g40: ز داد و ز بخشش are source/instrument phrases ('by/from justice and by/from generosity') using the poetic preposition ز. پر از خواسته is a predicate 'full of خواسته' where پر ('full') takes the complement introduced by از. خواسته here means 'wishes/requests'; the line says that because of justice and generosity the earth was filled with (people's) wants/wishes (i.e., their needs or desires were present/attended to).
segments.s179-g89: نهادند is an archaic/perhaps poetically colored past plural of نهادن used here as a transitive/causative-like verb meaning 'set' or 'turn' (i.e. 'turned'). The phrase places the directional adjunct سوی فرامرز between the verb and the noun روی (face), so the literal ordering is 'they turned toward Faramarz (their) face' = 'they turned their faces toward Faramarz.'
segments.s179-g90: گرفتند here is past plural from گرفتن used idiomatically: 'to be seized by / to feel' (they were seized by joy). به دیدار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'at/upon the sight of him' (اوی is an old pronominal form). The construction is effectively 'joy took hold of them at seeing him.'
segments.s179-g91: وزان is an archaic compound (و از آن) meaning 'and/from that (place/time)'; it sets the starting point. جایگه = 'place' and سوی شاه marks the direction 'toward the king.' آمدند is the past plural verb 'they came' and appears at the end, following the classical epic word order.
segments.s179-g92: به دیدار introduces purpose/direction 'to meet / to the presence of.' فرخ کلاه here functions as a proper name or epithet (literally 'fortunate hat' but used as a name/title) and آمدند repeats the past plural 'they came.' The sequence means 'they came to visit Farrokh Kolah.'
segments.s179-g93: چو = 'when.' خسرو here denotes the sovereign ('the king'); گو is an archaic/epic element attached to the name (an epithet or linking particle often found in classical Persian) and does not function as the main verb here. پیلتن را uses the object marker را to mark Piltan as the (direct) object, and بدید is the past singular 'saw.'
segments.s179-g94: سرشکش ('a little tear' / 'teardrop') is the grammatical subject; ز مژگان = 'from (his) eyelashes' (ز = from, مژگان = eyelashes). به رخ بر چکید is a directional phrase 'fell upon the cheek' with چکید (past of چکیدن) 'dripped/fell' at the end, so the full meaning is 'a teardrop from the eyelashes dripped onto the cheek.'
segments.s179-g95: فرود آمد is a compound verbal expression meaning 'he descended' or 'alighted' (فرود + آمد). از تخت specifies the source 'from the throne.' و کرد آفرین is a coordinated clause 'and he made/sounded “āfarīn”' where کرد + آفرین = 'uttered an acclamation / cried “bravo.”'
segments.s179-g96: تهمتن is the subject/name (Tahmatan); ببوسید is past singular 'kissed' (here 'kissed' the ground). روی زمین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the earth/ground' where روی is used in the sense 'on/upon.' The clause reads 'Tahmatan kissed the ground.'
segments.s179-g73: یکی لشکر = 'one army' or 'one of the armies'; numerical/determinative word یکی precedes the noun as in Persian. از جای is an adverbial phrase 'from (their) place' and برخاستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they rose/rose up' (بر-خاست + -ند), agreeing with the plural view of the host.
segments.s179-g74: پذیره شدن را is a nominal object phrase: پذیره (the reception/receiving) + شدن as a light/verbal noun 'being received' or 'the act of receiving', with را marking it as definite object. بیاراستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they arranged/arrayed/put in order' (bi- + آراستن), so the whole clause means 'they set about arranging the reception.'
segments.s179-g75: ز پهلو به پهلو is an adverbial phrase 'from flank to flank / side by side' (ز ... به ...). پذیره شدند uses the noun پذیره with شدند (past plural of شدن) to mean 'they were set to receive / took up the reception positions'—an intransitive/predicative use where شدند marks the past plural state.
segments.s179-g76: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject and با introduces accompaniment/instrument 'with'. درفش و تبیره are the items they carry; شدند is again past plural 'they were' or 'they became'—here 'they were furnished/arrayed with'. The clause describes that everyone had the banner and the tabīreh.
segments.s179-g77: برفتند is past plural 'they went/marched on'. پیشش (پیش + enclitic -ش) means 'toward him / before him' and functions adverbially. به دو روزه راه is a compound adverbial 'on a road of two days / along a two‑day journey' (دو روزه = 'two‑day' modifying راه). Note the verb + adverbial + subject order — the subject follows the verb for emphasis.
segments.s179-g78: چنین modifies پهلوانان: 'such champions/heroic men'; و connects to the following noun phrase; چندین سپاه = 'several/many hosts' (چندین as quantifier + سپاه 'army/host'). These noun phrases are postposed after the verb phrase for stylistic emphasis.
segments.s179-g79: درفش تهمتن is a genitival/name compound 'the standard/banner of Tahmātan' (epithetic name following the noun). چو here is the temporal 'when' and آمد پدید is an inverted verbal phrase 'came into view / appeared' (آمد 'came' + پدید 'manifest/appear').
segments.s179-g80: به خورشید is a simile/phrase 'like the sun' or 'toward the sun' introduced by به; گرد سپه uses گرد as 'dust' (گردِ سپه = 'the army's dust/dust of the host') and بردمید is an archaic past 3rd-person singular meaning 'lifted/blew up / carried off'. Together: 'it, like the sun, lifted the army's dust.'
segments.s179-g113: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چو (when). وزان پس is a two-part discourse marker = 'after that/then'; پس is an adverb 'then'. از تخت is a prepositional phrase 'from the throne', and برخاستند is a past-tense 3rd-person plural verb 'they rose/rose up', placed at the end as is typical in Persian main/subordinate clauses.
segments.s179-g114: نهادند is a past-tense transitive verb (3rd pl.) 'they placed/they set (out)'. خوان is its object 'the banquet/table of food'. و simply coordinates the next verb. می‌آراستند is the imperfective/past habitual form of آراستن, 'they were adorning/they adorned,' showing an ongoing or repeated action concurrent with نهادند.
segments.s179-g115: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign/king'. تا + temporal phrase ('نیمی از شب' = 'half of the night') marks the duration/limit, and نخفت is the past negative verb (ن + خفت) meaning 'did not sleep'. Word order places the verb at the end, as normal in Persian.
segments.s179-g116: گذشته modifies سخنها ('the past [stories/talks]') and together functions as the object/topic of بازگفت, a past-tense verb meaning 'he retold' or 'recounted again'. همه simply emphasizes 'all'. The implied subject is the same جهاندار from the previous clause.
segments.s179-g117: This is a set of imperatives and prohibitions. بخوبی بیارای is an exhortation 'adorn (yourself) well' (bi‑ + verb form gives the imperative/subjunctive coloring); فردا مگوی is a negative imperative 'do not say “tomorrow”'. The colon introduces direct counsel: خود را ... آراسته کن is a transitive imperative 'make/adorn yourself with the virtues' (را marks the direct object خود), and اندوه فردا نداشته باش combines the negative participle نداشته with باش to form a prohibitive/subjunctive 'do not have sorrow for tomorrow.'
segments.s179-g118: بیاموختش is a past tense verb with the clitic object -ش, here best read as 'he taught him' (archaic usage of بیاموخت). The following nouns بزم و رزم و خرد enumerate what was taught: 'feasting, combat, and wisdom.' خواست is a past-tense verb 'he desired/wished.' The remainder expresses that he wished to take/carry him on a day of repose and that he wanted (می‌خواست) him to accompany (همراهی کند) him on a joyful day; که introduces the subordinate clause of desire. Note that some orthography/spacing here is compressed/archaic, but the verbs خواست and می‌خواست express past wishes and همراهی کند is a subjunctive infinitival clause 'that he accompany.'
segments.s179-g65: Word order is archaic: دل شاه (the king's heart) is the subject, شد (became) is the past copular/inasmuch-as 'become', and شادمان is the predicate adjective 'glad'. زان سخن is an adverbial phrase 'from/by that speech' placed between verb and adjective; together it yields 'the king's heart became glad because of those words.'
segments.s179-g66: سراینده را is the marked (definite) object/indirect object 'to the poet' (را here flags the person addressed). گفت is the past verb 'said' (here introducing the king's words). کاباد مان is the quoted string he utters; its exact lexical parsing is uncertain in the supplied senses, so it is treated as a quoted name/wish and left untranslated except as a transliteration.
segments.s179-g67: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that'), followed by a copular construction: اویست functions as a copula ('he is' in this older fused form) and is followed by the two role-nouns پروردگار پدر 'nurturer/lord and father' describing the person referred to. (The supplied lemma senses do not list a plain copula for اویست, so its function is described rather than tied to a listed sense.)
segments.s179-g68: وزویست is a compact classical formula meaning roughly 'and it is from him' (و + ز + وی + است): it attributes origin to 'him'. پیدا is predicate 'apparent/manifest'. به گیتی is the locative phrase 'in the world', and هنر is the noun 'skill/art' governed by the predicate: 'and from him skill is manifest in the world.'
segments.s179-g69: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning 'he ordered/commanded/said (in the sense of ordering)'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('that' / 'so that'), and the following names گیو و گودرز و طوس are the coordinated subjects of the subordinate verb (the men ordered to go).
segments.s179-g70: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they went / departed'. با marks accompaniment or instrument 'with', here listing instruments of departure: نای رویین 'the (iron) reed (flute)' and کوس 'the war-drum'. رویین modifies نای ('iron/steeled ney'). The coordination with و links the two instruments.
segments.s179-g71: تبیره is the nominative subject meaning 'interpretation/announcement/proclamation' here; برآمد is the past intransitive 'arose/issued forth'. ز درگاه شاه is the source phrase 'from the king's court/royal presence' (ز = 'from', درگاه شاه = 'the king's court').
segments.s179-g72: همه 'all' is the subject for the following past plural verb برنهادند 'they raised/displayed'. گردان کلاه is the object phrase: کلاه 'hat/helmet' is the concrete noun; گردان here modifies it in an older/poetic sense and its exact nuance is unclear from the provided senses, so the compound is understood idiomatically as 'their helmets' (i.e. they lifted their helmets in response).
segments.s179-g17: ازین هر سه = از + این (classical orthography) + هر سه; literally 'of these, all three'. The clause ends with بود مایه‌دار, where بود (past copula) follows the subject and mایه‌دار is a predicate adjective meaning 'richly endowed' or 'having substance'. Word order is topical (ازین هر سه گوهر) + predicate (بود مایه‌دار).
segments.s179-g18: که introduces a clause of explanation/reason here ('for' / 'because'), followed by زیبا بود (adjective + past copula) meaning 'was beautiful'. خلعت کردگار is a genitive phrase 'the robe/gift of the Creator' (خلعت = robe of honour, کردگار = the Creator/God). The clause explains or qualifies the previous statement.
segments.s179-g19: چو functions as a conditional/subordinate conjunction ('if' / 'when'); هر سه is 'the three (all three)'. بیابی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive/optative form of یافتن 'to obtain/find' — here 'if/when you find/attain'. خرد (a noun 'wisdom') is the logical subject/predicate of the following predicate بایدت: بایدت = باید + enclitic ت ('it is necessary for you' / 'you must have').
segments.s179-g20: شناسنده is a present-participle/noun 'one who knows/recognizes' and here is the predicate (you must be a recognizer). نیک و بد is the coordinated object/contrast 'good and bad'. The final بایدت repeats the necessity form 'is required of you/you must'. Word order: predicate (شناسنده نیک و بد) + modal necessity (بایدت).
segments.s179-g21: چو = 'when/if'. این چار = 'these four' (چار = four, i.e., the four qualities/things mentioned). با یک تن آید بهم is a classical phrasing: با یک تن = 'in/with one person' and آید بهم = 'come together' (آید = comes/happens; بهم = together / into one). The whole clause: 'when these four come together in one person.'
segments.s179-g22: براساید is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'will be freed/spared' or 'will fare/ be at ease' (here passive/impersonal sense 'one will be rid'). It takes a from-phrase از ... to mark what one is spared: از آز وز رنج و غم = 'from greed (آز), and (وز used as a classical linker/weight) from burden, toil and sorrow.' وز is archaic here as a linking/weight word accompanying رنج و غم rather than its ordinary literal senses.
segments.s179-g23: مگر introduces an exception meaning 'except' (or 'but'). The following relative/causal clause کز مرگ خود چاره نیست uses کز (= classical 'که + ز' meaning 'from/for/that from') to mark cause/relation: 'for/from death itself there is no remedy.' خود is emphatic 'itself' or 'own'. چاره نیست is 'there is no remedy/solution.' Word order: exception + explanatory relative clause.
segments.s179-g24: وزین is an archaic/poetic adjective/adverb here conveying 'weighty/grievous' (literally 'heavy' in sense), followed by بدتر 'worse' and a comparative construction از بخت پتیاره ('than the fate/fortune pتیاره' — پتیاره is an obsolete word meaning a wretched/ill-starred lot). The final نیست negates the whole clause: 'there is nothing worse than a grievous/wretched fate.'
segments.s179-g1: به پالیز: a locative phrase 'in/at the orchard' with به used to mark place. چون introduces a temporal clause 'when'. برکشد is the classical present/optative form of برکشیدن 'to raise/lift' (here simple present/gnomic: 'lifts'). سرو شاخ is a compact object–predicate order: 'the cypress (سرو) (lifts) a bough (شاخ)' — Persian often places the noun and its object close together in poetry.
segments.s179-g2: سر شاخ سبزش: 'the head/top (سر) of the bough, its green part' — سبزش is سبز + enclitic -ش 'its', referring back to the cypress/bough. برآید is the present/poetic 'appear/arise'. ز کاخ = 'from the palace' with poetic preposition ز = از. Overall structure: noun phrase (its green top) + verb (arises) + source (from the palace).
segments.s179-g3: به بالای او: 'at its/his top' — بالای = 'top, summit of' and او is the 3rd-person pronoun. شاد باشد درخت: copula + predicate 'be happy' (باشد used as present/canonical copula) with درخت (the tree) placed after the verb for poetic inversion: 'the tree is happy at its top.'
segments.s179-g4: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بیندش is the verb بیندَن 'he sees' with enclitic -ش 'it' (object pronoun referring to the tree). بینادل is a rare/uncertain reading (an epithet here describing the seer). و links the two epithets; نیک‌بخت is 'fortunate' or 'well-fated' — both epithets modify the person who sees the tree.
segments.s179-g5: سزد‌گر is a conditional/optative formula 'if it be fitting' or 'should one deem it proper' (گر as the conditional/agentive particle). گمانی 'a conjecture/guess' is the object; برد here is the verb 'takes/holds' in classical usage ('to take a guess' — poetically used). بر سه چیز = 'about/on three things' (بر marking the domain/topic).
segments.s179-g6: کزین = 'from these / of these' (poetic contraction of از + این). گذشتی is 2nd-person past 'you passed' (have passed/been past). چه = interrogative 'what/which'. چیزست is a fused existential/predicate 'it is a thing' used idiomatically here as 'what thing is it' → 'which of the things'. نیز functions as a connective 'also/then' adding emphasis to the question. Word order is poetic but yields: 'Of these three, which have you passed?'
segments.s179-g7: هنر is the subject 'art/virtue/excellence'. با نژادست = 'is with/by lineage' — نژادست is an archaic/fused predicative form meaning 'is of (one's) birth/lineage'; با + noun expresses 'by/with (means or possession)'. و با گوهر است repeats the pattern 'and is by/with essence' (گوهر here = 'essence' rather than just 'gem'). The final است is the copula for the second clause.
segments.s179-g8: سه چیزست = 'there are three things' (چیزست a fused existential/predicate). و هر سه = 'and each/all three' (هر سه = each of the three / the three together). به‌بنداندرست is a compound/poetic expression meaning 'is firmly bound/tightly joined' — the predicate describing the three items as firmly tied together; the copula is understood from the previous clause.
segments.s179-g9: This clause is a short rhetorical question. هنر (art/skill) is the subject, کی (here = 'when') introduces the temporal/conditional clause, and بود is the past copula ('was/was there'); تا + نباشد forms a subordinate negative clause meaning 'that there would not be' (تا here functions like 'that/so that' in older poetry). In other words: 'When there was/if there is art, that there would not be a jewel…' — the negated نباشد is a present/jussive form meaning 'is not/there is not.'
segments.s179-g10: This is a separate statement following the question. نژاده ('well‑born / noble') is used as a nominal description, بسی here functions adverbially ('much/often' in older usage), دیده‌ای is the perfect/experience form 'you have seen', and بی‌هنر is an adjectival phrase 'without skill' modifying the nobles. Word order is pragmatic: noun (نژاده) + adverb (بسی) + verb (دیده‌ای) + predicate adjective (بی‌هنر).
segments.s179-g11: گهر is the head noun 'jewel'. آنک is a literary/relative particle 'which/that' introducing a relative clause; از + فر یزدان means 'from the (divine) farr' (فر = royal/divine glory, یزدان = divine power/God). بود is the copula ('was / is' in the clause), forming 'a jewel that is from the divine farr.'
segments.s179-g12: نیازد is a negative present form of نیازیدن used here to mean 'does not need' (the lexical sense 'to need' is in play). به indicates the indirect object/beneficiary ('to/for'), بد دست is a compound meaning 'bad/awkward hand' (difficult or unskillful handling), و joins the clauses, and بد نشنود is another negative present meaning 'does not hear/listen to bad' — idiomatically 'does not tolerate/accept evil or ill treatment.' The verbs are third‑person predicates referring back to the jewel.
segments.s179-g13: نژاد ('lineage') is the topic, and آنک again is the relative particle 'that/which.' باشد is the present/subjunctive copula here functioning as 'is/comes to be' in literary register, ز (poetic form of از) marks 'from', تخم پدر is a genitive phrase 'the father's seed' (takhm = seed/origin, پدر = father/ancestor). The clause reads 'a lineage which is from the father's seed.'
segments.s179-g14: سزد is an impersonal present 'it befits/it is proper.' کاید here is a noun meaning 'plot/cunning' (an older poetic usage). از تخم پاکیزه is a prepositional phrase 'from a pure seed/stock' and بر is an archaic/post‑positional particle meaning roughly 'forth/upon' used here to complete the verbal idea 'spring forth.' Together: 'it befits that cunning should spring from a pure seed.'
segments.s179-g15: هنرگر ('craftsman/adept') is a vocative/topic. بیاموزی is an optative/subjunctive form meaning 'may you learn' or 'learn (you should)' — a polite exhortation. از هر کسی is a simple prepositional phrase 'from everyone/anyone' (هر 'every/any', کسی 'person/anyone'). The clause thus says 'craftsman, learn from everyone.'
segments.s179-g16: بکوشی is an exhortative/subjunctive verb 'strive/endeavor (you should)'. و simply coordinates 'and'. پیچی is a noun/adjectival form here meaning 'cunning/skill' (literary), ز رنجش بسی is an ablative phrase 'from much suffering' (ز = from, رنجش = suffering, بسی = much/many). The whole clause: 'strive and, through much suffering, attain/turn to cunning.'
segments.s179-g49: که introduces a subordinate/time clause 'when/that'. The clause has subject (خسرو) followed by a pair of prepositional phrases 'ز توران به ایران' ('from Turan into Iran') and ends with the past verb رسید 'arrived'. Word order (subject + complements + verb) is typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s179-g50: نشست is the past of نشستن 'sat' (verb placed before its complements in this poetic line). از بر تخت is a classical/poetic locative phrase meaning 'upon the throne' (از + بر is an archaic/epic combination). کو را سزید is a short clause meaning 'which/what was deserved' or 'as was fitting' — را is the definite/object marker attached to the relative/interrogative element (here marking what is due), and سزید (from سزیدن) means 'to merit / to befit'.
segments.s179-g51: بیاراست is a past tense/preterite verb 'he adorned/arrayed (himself)' placed before the name (poetic inversion). رستم is the subject. به دیدار شاه is a purpose/directional phrase 'for the meeting/ audience with the king' (به + noun). The verb is transitive/reflective here: Rostam prepared himself.
segments.s179-g52: ببیند is a subjunctive/optative form of دیدن used to express purpose 'in order to see'. که تا هست functions as a temporal/concessive connector 'while/as long as there is', where تا + هست = 'while/so long as'. زیبای گاه is a nominal phrase 'the auspicious/beautiful hour' (زیبای used adjectivally). Overall this gives the purpose: 'to see (the king) while the favourable moment remains.'
segments.s179-g53: This segment is a coordinated list of personal names followed by بهم 'together'. Grammatically it is a nominative list (names enumerated in sequence); بهم marks their being 'together' or 'as one'. The first token (ابا) is unclear here in the provided senses and is treated as a lexical item/name in the list.
segments.s179-g54: بزرگان کابل is a genitive construction 'the great men/chiefs of Kabul'. همه 'all' quantifies them, and the idiom بیش و کم 'more and less' means 'more or less / one and all'. Word order is noun + genitive + quantification + idiomatic phrase.
segments.s179-g55: که شد introduces a temporal/relative clause 'when/that it became'. سپاهی is the head noun 'the army/host'. شد is the past of شدن 'became' or 'came to be', and دشت چون آبنوس is a simile 'the plain like ebony' (چون = 'like/as'). The clause thus says that when the army was present the plain appeared as black as ebony (poetic hyperbole).
segments.s179-g56: بدرید is the past of دریدن 'to tear' used vividly: the verb comes before its object (poetic order). هر گوش 'every ear' is the object, and ز اوای کوس 'from the sound of the drum' gives the cause/instrument ('from the blast/sound of the war‑drum'). The literal image is 'the drum‑sound tore every ear' = the drums were deafening.
segments.s179-g25: This is a noun phrase identifying the subject: جهانجوی (the world-seeker / ambitious man) followed by a prepositional/partitive phrase از این چار بد meaning “from/of these four evils.” چار here means “four” (a list of four misfortunes just implied) and بی‌نیاز is a predicate/adjectival phrase meaning “free of / untroubled by.” Word order is noun + از-phrase + adjectival predicate; the copula is omitted here and supplied later by بود in the next segment.
segments.s179-g26: This is the main predicate for the preceding subject. همش (adverb) = “all / entirely,” بخت = “fortune/destiny,” سازنده functions as a noun here (“maker/one who makes” or poetically ‘that which shapes’), بود is the past copula “was,” and از فراز is a prepositional phrase “from on high / from above.” The line literally says his whole fortune was made/ordained on high; order is adverb → noun → modifier → copula → source phrase.
segments.s179-g27: سخن راند here is a poetic expression meaning “the speech / narrative moved on” (راند used in the sense ‘drove forward / continued’). گویا is a parenthetical “it seems / apparently.” بدین داستان (“in/with this tale”) is one unit meaning “in this story”; the provided segmentation into ب + دین appears to be a mistake in the word list — here بدین functions as a single prepositional/adverbial phrase “by/with this.”
segments.s179-g28: دگر here is an adverb “again,” and گوید is the verb “(he) says / relates” in the narrative/present habitual used for telling stories. از گفته باستان = “from/about the ancient account” (از = “about/of,” گفته = “what is told / the account,” باستان = “ancient”). Word order is adverb → verb → prepositional phrase giving the source/topic of the saying.
segments.s179-g29: کنون “now” + بازگردم is a 1st-person present-subjunctive/optative form meaning “I return” (classical-narrative: “I return now…” / “let me return”), followed by the prepositional phrase به آغاز کار “to the beginning of the affair/work.” This is a narrative formula used to resume the main thread.
segments.s179-g30: که introduces the subordinate clause (’to say/that’). چون here means “how,” بود is the past copula “was,” کردار = “deed / conduct,” and آن شهریار = “that king.” Together: “that (I may tell) how the conduct of that king was.” Note the relative/indirect question sense introduced by که + چون.
segments.s179-g31: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” تاج بزرگی is the noun phrase “a great crown.” بسر برنهاد is a classical compound meaning “placed upon the head” (بسَر = “on the head” + برنهاد = “placed on”); the provided senses for بسر do not match this usage (see note). The whole segment describes the action ‘when he put the great crown on his head.’
segments.s179-g32: This has a slightly inverted order for emphasis: ازو “because of him / from him,” شاد شد تاج = “the crown became glad” (verb before subject), و او نیز شاد = “and he too was glad.” شاد شد is the past/perfect idea ‘became happy.’ The inversion (verb then noun) is common in poetry for rhythm or emphasis.

sentences.s179-s29: They took the road toward the city of Iran: Zovāreh, Faramarz, the elephant, and the army.
sentences.s179-s30: Ahead, inside, Zal with his assembly; the purple standard came from behind Piltan after the elephants.
sentences.s179-s31: Then news reached the king that a mounted champion had come along the road.
sentences.s179-s32: Zovāreh, Faramarz, and Dastan, Sam — great men who are endowed with rank and fame.
sentences.s179-s41: A clamor rose and the blasts of horn and drum — from the heart of the host: Giv, Goudarz, and Tus.
sentences.s179-s42: They drove toward the seer Piltan; with joy they cried 'Go!' and chanted 'bravo.'
sentences.s179-s43: They seized all three and set them aside; Shirāvazhan asked the king.
sentences.s179-s44: They came from Rostam to Zal and Sam; they arrived open‑hearted and fortunate.
sentences.s179-s53: Rostam looked; the cypress‑like one at his feet sat down, spoke, and gave his counsel.
sentences.s179-s54: Rakhsh became bloodied and his heart was full of pain; because of Siyāvash's affair he remembered many things.
sentences.s179-s55: He said to the king of the world: 'O sovereign! You are the world’s (ruler) and a remembrance of your father.'
sentences.s179-s56: I have not seen in the world a crown‑bearer with such glory and likeness to his father.
sentences.s179-s49: He said to Rostam: “O champion, may you always be joyful and bright‑souled.”
sentences.s179-s50: In the world you are wise and silent, for you are the protector of Siavash.
sentences.s179-s51: Zal then took him into his arms; for the father's sake he laid his hand on his head.
sentences.s179-s52: He set him upon the moon‑like throne, and for them he invoked the name of God.
sentences.s179-s21: When Jam and Fereydun adorned the age, the king never rested from justice and generosity.
sentences.s179-s22: The world became full of goodness and safety; the demonic hand was bound from evil.
sentences.s179-s23: Envoys came from every direction, from every renowned source and every quarter.
sentences.s179-s24: Then the report came to Nimruz, to the presence of the commander Giti-froz.
sentences.s179-s17: He rebuilt every ruined place; he freed the hearts of the sorrowful from grief.
sentences.s179-s18: Dew fell from the spring clouds; from the face of the earth he removed the stain (rust) of grief.
sentences.s179-s19: The world became full of greenery and of rivers of water; sleep came upon the heads of the sorrowful.
sentences.s179-s20: The earth became adorned like a paradise; through justice and through generosity it was full of desires (wishes).
sentences.s179-s45: They turned their faces toward Faramarz; joy seized them at the sight of him.
sentences.s179-s46: From that place they came toward the king; they came to the presence of Farrokh Kolah.
sentences.s179-s47: When the king saw Piltan, a teardrop fell from his eyelash upon his cheek.
sentences.s179-s48: He descended from the throne and cried 'Bravo!'; Tahmatan kissed the ground.
sentences.s179-s37: One of the armies rose from its place and prepared the reception.
sentences.s179-s38: They took up positions side by side; everyone was equipped with banner and tabīreh.
sentences.s179-s39: Such champions and several hosts marched forward toward him on a two‑day road.
sentences.s179-s40: When Tahmātan's standard appeared, it came to view and, like the sun, raised the army's dust.
sentences.s179-s57: After that, when they rose from the throne, they set out the banquet and adorned it.
sentences.s179-s58: The sovereign did not sleep until half the night; he retold all the past tales.
sentences.s179-s59: Adorn yourself well and do not say 'tomorrow': clothe yourself in virtues and have no sorrow for the coming day. He taught him feasting and battle and wisdom; he wished to take him on a day of rest and wanted that he accompany him on a day of rejoicing.
sentences.s179-s33: The king's heart was made glad by those words; he said to the poet, "Kābād mān."
sentences.s179-s34: For he is the lord and father, and from him skill is made manifest in the world.
sentences.s179-s35: He commanded that Giv, Goudarz, and Tūs go forth, taking the iron reed and the drum.
sentences.s179-s36: A proclamation rose from the king's court; all raised their helmets.
sentences.s179-s9: Each of these three jewels was richly endowed, for the Creator's robe was beautiful.
sentences.s179-s10: If you gain all three, wisdom is required of you; you must be one who recognizes good and bad.
sentences.s179-s11: When these four come together in one person, he will be rid of greed, burden, toil, and sorrow.
sentences.s179-s12: Except death — for there is no remedy against death itself — and nothing is worse than a grievous fate.
sentences.s179-s1: When the cypress in the orchard lifts a bough, the green tip of that bough rises up from the palace.
sentences.s179-s2: The tree is joyful at its summit; when the noble-hearted and the fortunate one sees it.
sentences.s179-s3: If one were to venture a guess, it would concern three things; of these three, which have you passed?
sentences.s179-s4: Excellence lies in lineage and in essence; there are three things, and all three are firmly bound together.
sentences.s179-s5: When art exists, is there not a jewel? You have seen many well‑born people who lack skill.
sentences.s179-s6: A jewel that comes from the divine farr needs no clumsy hand and does not hearken to evil.
sentences.s179-s7: A lineage that comes from the father's seed — it is fitting that cunning should spring from a pure stock.
sentences.s179-s8: O craftsman, learn from everyone; strive, and by much suffering become cunning.
sentences.s179-s25: When Khosrow came from Turan to Iran, he sat upon the throne as was fitting.
sentences.s179-s26: Rostam arrayed himself for an audience with the king, to see him while the auspicious hour still lasted.
sentences.s179-s27: Aba, Zal, Sam, Nariman together — the chiefs of Kabul, all of them, more or less.
sentences.s179-s28: When the army spread across the plain and the plain looked like ebony, the blast of the war‑drums split every ear.
sentences.s179-s13: The world-seeker, untouched by these four evils, had all his fortune made on high.
sentences.s179-s14: The tale proceeds thus — it seems — and again tells from the ancient account.
sentences.s179-s15: Now I return to the beginning of the tale, to say how that king acted.
sentences.s179-s16: When he set a great crown upon his head, the crown was gladdened by him, and he too was glad.
